fields.bio: Platform admin and tech enthusiast. I keep the lights on around here.
Co-admin of Rare Publishing. Music producer by night, moderator by day.
Full-stack developer writing about React, Python, and everything in between.
Travel writer and amateur photographer. Currently exploring the Southeast.
Home cook turned food blogger. Nashville hot chicken is my love language.
Book reviewer and aspiring novelist. If I'm not reading, I'm probably napping.
Personal trainer and wellness advocate. Let's get moving!
Indie game developer and retro gaming collector. Currently obsessed with roguelikes.
Environmental scientist blogging about sustainability and green living.
Music journalist covering indie, jazz, and everything your algorithm won't recommend.
Serial entrepreneur. Three startups in, still optimistic. Writing about the hustle.
Photographer and visual storyteller based in Nashville. Mostly landscapes and street.

fields.title: Why I Switched from REST to GraphQL (And Then Switched Back)
fields.content: After six months of running GraphQL in production, I have some thoughts. The developer experience is fantastic during development — the self-documenting schema, the ability to request exactly what you need, and the playground for testing queries. But the operational complexity caught up with us. Caching became a nightmare, error handling was inconsistent, and our backend team spent more time writing resolvers than actual business logic. For our team size and use case, REST with good documentation turned out to be the pragmatic choice. GraphQL is powerful, but it's not always the right tool.

fields.title: React Server Components: A Practical Guide for Skeptics
fields.content: I was skeptical about React Server Components when they were first announced. Another paradigm shift? Really? But after building a production app with Next.js App Router, I'm cautiously optimistic. The mental model is different — you have to think about which components need interactivity and which can render on the server. The performance gains are real though. Our initial page load dropped by 40% and our bundle size shrunk significantly. Here's what I learned along the way and the gotchas that tripped me up.

fields.title: The Junior Developer's Guide to Reading Legacy Code
fields.content: You've just joined a new team and they've pointed you at a codebase that's been around since 2018. There are no tests, the README is outdated, and half the dependencies are deprecated. Sound familiar? Here's my survival guide. First, resist the urge to rewrite everything. That codebase works — it's making money. Second, start with the entry points: where do requests come in? Follow the data flow. Third, git blame is your best friend. Find out who wrote what and ask them why. Fourth, write tests for the parts you need to change before you change them.

fields.title: Building a CLI Tool with Python: From Script to Package
fields.content: What started as a 50-line script to rename my photo files turned into a proper CLI tool with argument parsing, progress bars, and error handling. Here's how I went from a messy script to a publishable Python package. We'll cover argparse vs click vs typer, how to structure your project, writing tests for CLI tools, and publishing to PyPI. The whole journey took about a weekend, and now my team uses it daily.

fields.title: Django vs FastAPI: Choosing the Right Python Backend in 2026
fields.content: I've built production applications with both Django and FastAPI, and the 'which is better' debate misses the point entirely. They're optimized for different things. Django gives you an admin panel, ORM, authentication, and a massive ecosystem out of the box. FastAPI gives you speed, modern Python type hints, and async support. If you're building a content-heavy application with user management, Django is hard to beat. If you're building a microservice or API-first product, FastAPI shines. Here's my detailed comparison from real-world usage.

fields.title: 48 Hours in Asheville: A Food and Nature Lover's Guide
fields.content: Asheville has been on my list for years, and it did not disappoint. Day one started with breakfast at Biscuit Head (get the cathead biscuit with gravy), followed by a hike on the Blue Ridge Parkway. The views from Craggy Gardens are worth the drive alone. For lunch, we hit up 12 Bones Smokehouse — Obama ate here twice for a reason. The afternoon was spent wandering the River Arts District, where you can watch artists work in their studios. Day two was all about the Biltmore Estate and downtown breweries. Here's the full itinerary with costs.

fields.title: Budget Travel in Japan: What Nobody Tells You
fields.content: Everyone says Japan is expensive. They're wrong — or at least, they're not looking hard enough. I spent three weeks in Japan for less than I'd spend on two weeks in most European cities. The secret? Konbini (convenience store) meals are genuinely delicious and cost $3-5. Capsule hotels are an experience and half the price of regular hotels. The Japan Rail Pass pays for itself after two long-distance trips. Local izakayas are cheaper than tourist restaurants and the food is better. Here are my actual daily expenses broken down by city.

fields.title: The Natchez Trace: America's Most Underrated Road Trip
fields.content: Four hundred and forty-four miles of pure, uninterrupted scenic road from Nashville to Natchez, Mississippi. No commercial traffic, no billboards, no stoplights. The Natchez Trace Parkway is a national treasure that most people have never heard of. I drove the entire thing over three days, stopping at mounds built by Indigenous peoples a thousand years ago, civil war sites, and some of the most beautiful waterfall hikes in the South. If you live in Nashville and haven't done this drive, what are you waiting for?

fields.title: Solo Travel Isn't Lonely — It's Liberating
fields.content: I used to think solo travel was something people did because they couldn't find anyone to go with. Then I spent two weeks alone in Portugal and realized it was the most freeing experience of my life. You eat when you're hungry, sleep when you're tired, and change plans without consulting anyone. I had conversations with strangers I never would have met traveling with friends. The initial discomfort of eating alone at a restaurant fades quickly. Here's what I learned about myself and some practical tips for first-time solo travelers.

fields.title: Nashville Hot Chicken at Home: The Real Deal
fields.content: After testing 47 different recipes and burning my taste buds off more times than I can count, I've finally nailed Nashville hot chicken at home. The secret isn't just cayenne — it's the combination of cayenne, paprika, garlic powder, and a touch of brown sugar in the paste. And you need lard, not butter. The chicken should be brined overnight, dredged in seasoned flour, and fried at exactly 325°F. The paste goes on RIGHT when it comes out of the oil. Serve on white bread with pickles. Accept no substitutions.

fields.title: Meal Prep Sunday: A Week of Lunches in 2 Hours
fields.content: I used to spend $15 a day on lunch. That's $75 a week, $300 a month, and a truly embarrassing amount per year. Now I spend Sunday afternoons prepping five lunches for about $30 total. This week's menu: Mediterranean grain bowls with chicken, roasted vegetables, and tzatziki. The key to meal prep that you'll actually eat is variety in toppings and sauces, even when the base is the same. I'll walk you through my exact process, grocery list, and storage tips so nothing goes soggy.

fields.title: The Science of Perfect Scrambled Eggs
fields.content: Gordon Ramsay says low and slow. J. Kenji López-Alt says medium heat with constant stirring. My grandmother says high heat and confidence. After scrambling approximately one million eggs, here's what I've found: the temperature debate matters less than two other factors — the freshness of the eggs and when you add the salt. Salt your eggs 15 minutes before cooking and the proteins break down slightly, resulting in more tender curds. Farm-fresh eggs with those deep orange yolks are a game changer. Here's the complete breakdown with side-by-side comparisons.

fields.title: Why Your Homemade Bread Fails (And How to Fix It)
fields.content: You followed the recipe exactly. You kneaded for the right amount of time. You let it rise in a warm spot. And it still came out dense and sad. I've been there. After two years of baking bread almost daily, here are the five most common mistakes: your water is too hot and killing the yeast, you're measuring flour by volume instead of weight, you're not developing the gluten enough, your oven isn't actually the temperature it says it is, and you're cutting into it too soon. Let's fix each one.

fields.title: The 10 Books That Changed How I Think About Software
fields.content: Not all of these are programming books, and that's the point. 'Thinking in Systems' by Donella Meadows taught me more about architecture than any tech book. 'The Design of Everyday Things' changed how I think about user interfaces. 'Sapiens' helped me understand why organizations make irrational decisions. Of course, 'Clean Code' and 'Pragmatic Programmer' are on here too. But the most impactful book was 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' — understanding cognitive biases made me a better code reviewer and a more empathetic teammate.

fields.title: Why I Read 50 Pages of Every Book Before Deciding to Finish It
fields.content: Life is too short for books you're not enjoying. I used to force myself to finish every book I started — some misguided sense of duty. Now I give every book exactly 50 pages. If it hasn't grabbed me by then, I move on guilt-free. This simple rule tripled my reading volume because I stopped spending weeks slogging through books I didn't like. Some books I've abandoned have great reputations. Some books I've devoured were random finds. Trust your own taste.

fields.title: Sci-Fi That Predicted Our Present: A Reading List
fields.content: Orwell gave us surveillance states. Huxley gave us distraction as control. But some sci-fi predictions are eerily specific. Neal Stephenson's 'Snow Crash' (1992) predicted the metaverse, avatar-based social interaction, and gig economy delivery drivers. Octavia Butler's 'Parable of the Sower' (1993) predicted climate refugees and gated communities. William Gibson coined 'cyberspace' before the internet was mainstream. Here are twelve sci-fi novels that look less like fiction every year, organized by theme.

fields.title: The Only 5 Exercises You Actually Need
fields.content: The fitness industry wants to sell you complexity. New equipment, new programs, new supplements every month. But the truth is boring: if you can squat, hinge, push, pull, and carry, you're covering every movement pattern your body needs. A barbell squat, a deadlift, a bench press, a pull-up, and a farmer's carry. That's it. Master these five movements and you'll be stronger, more mobile, and more functional than 90% of gym-goers doing Instagram workouts. Here's why each one matters and how to progress.

fields.title: Why Your Morning Run Isn't Helping You Lose Weight
fields.content: You've been running three miles every morning for six months and the scale hasn't budged. Here's the uncomfortable truth: steady-state cardio is terrible for fat loss when it's your only strategy. Your body adapts quickly to repetitive cardio, burning fewer calories over time for the same effort. Worse, it can increase cortisol and appetite, leading you to eat back more than you burned. I'm not saying don't run — running has amazing cardiovascular benefits. But if fat loss is the goal, you need to add resistance training and look at your nutrition.

fields.title: Sleep Is the Most Underrated Performance Enhancer
fields.content: I can tell within five minutes of a training session whether my client slept well the night before. Strength drops 10-15%, reaction time slows, and injury risk skyrockets on poor sleep. Yet most people will spend $200 on supplements while sleeping six hours on a bad mattress. Here's what the research says: 7-9 hours in a cool, dark room with consistent sleep and wake times will do more for your fitness than any supplement, program, or gadget. I'll break down the science and give you a sleep optimization checklist.

fields.title: The Golden Age of Indie Games Is Right Now
fields.content: Forget AAA releases with their $70 price tags and day-one patches. The best gaming experiences of 2025 came from small studios with big ideas. 'Balatro' proved that a poker-themed roguelike could be game of the year. 'Animal Well' showed that a single developer can create a puzzle masterpiece. The tools are better than ever — Unity, Godot, and Unreal are all free to start with. Distribution through Steam and itch.io means you don't need a publisher. We're living in the golden age and most people are too busy complaining about the latest AAA disappointment to notice.

fields.title: Building My First Game in Godot: Lessons from 6 Months
fields.content: Six months ago I decided to build a game. I had programming experience but zero game dev knowledge. I chose Godot over Unity for its open-source nature and GDScript's Python-like syntax. The first month was pure tutorials. The second month I started my own project and immediately realized tutorials teach you syntax, not architecture. Month three was a rewrite. By month four, I had something playable. Here's everything I wish someone had told me: start with a game jam scope, not your dream game. Use state machines. Playtest early and often.

fields.title: Why Retro Games Feel Better Than Modern Games
fields.content: This isn't nostalgia talking — I was born in 1998. I discovered retro games as an adult and I genuinely believe many of them are more fun than modern releases. The reason is constraints. When you only have 16 colors and 64KB of memory, every design decision matters. There's no filler content, no bloated open worlds, no microtransactions. A game like Mega Man 2 respects your time in a way that most modern 80-hour RPGs don't. The tight controls, immediate feedback loops, and clear progression make these games timeless. Here are my top 20 retro games that hold up perfectly.

fields.title: The Recycling Myth: What Actually Happens to Your Blue Bin
fields.content: I hate to be the bearer of bad news, but most of what you put in your recycling bin doesn't get recycled. National Geographic estimates that only 5-6% of plastic in the US is actually recycled. The rest goes to landfill, gets incinerated, or is shipped overseas. The recycling symbol on plastic doesn't mean it's recyclable — it's a resin identification code that the plastics industry deliberately designed to look like the recycling symbol. The real solution isn't better recycling — it's reducing consumption and demanding companies use less packaging.

fields.title: I Tried Zero-Waste Living for 30 Days. Here's What Happened.
fields.content: Day one: I'm motivated. I've got my mason jars, my reusable bags, my bamboo toothbrush. Day seven: I'm spending twice as long grocery shopping, visiting three different stores to find package-free options. Day fourteen: I cave and buy something in plastic because I'm exhausted. Day thirty: I've reduced my waste by about 60%, not 100%. And that's okay. The zero-waste movement's biggest flaw is the 'zero' part — it sets an impossible standard that makes people give up entirely. Reducing waste by half is still incredible. Here's what worked, what didn't, and what I'm keeping.

fields.title: Urban Composting: A Complete Guide for Apartment Dwellers
fields.content: You don't need a backyard to compost. I've been composting in my 600-square-foot apartment for two years using a combination of bokashi bins and vermicomposting (that's worms, for the uninitiated). The bokashi system handles meat and dairy — things traditional composting can't. The worm bin handles fruit and vegetable scraps and produces incredible fertilizer for my houseplants. The whole setup cost about $60 and takes up the space of a small trash can. No smell if you do it right. Here's my complete setup guide.

fields.title: Nashville's Underground Music Scene: Beyond Country
fields.content: Yes, Nashville is Music City. And yes, the honky-tonks on Broadway are fun. But if you think Nashville is just country music, you're missing 90% of what makes this city's music scene incredible. The garage rock coming out of East Nashville rivals anything from Detroit or Austin. The jazz scene at Rudy's Jazz Room is world-class. Grimey's New and Preloved Music isn't just a record store — it's a community hub with incredible in-store performances. Here's your guide to finding the real Nashville music scene, neighborhood by neighborhood.

fields.title: How Streaming Killed the Album (And Why That Matters)
fields.content: The average listener on Spotify plays 1.7 seconds of a song before deciding to skip. Artists now front-load hooks because if you don't grab attention in the first second, you've lost the stream. Albums used to be journeys — Dark Side of the Moon, OK Computer, To Pimp a Butterfly. They had pacing, themes, and intentional track ordering. Now artists release 20-track albums padded with filler because more tracks means more potential streams. We optimized for engagement metrics and lost the art form. Some artists are fighting back though, and they give me hope.

fields.title: My Startup Failed. Here's What I'd Do Differently.
fields.content: After 18 months and $120K of runway, we shut down FreshRoute, our farm-to-table delivery startup. The idea was solid, the execution was solid, but we made one fatal mistake: we didn't validate our pricing model before building the product. We assumed customers would pay a premium for locally-sourced groceries delivered to their door. They wouldn't. The convenience premium of existing delivery services was too strong. If I could go back, I'd spend the first three months doing nothing but customer interviews and pricing experiments. Build nothing. Validate everything. Your idea is probably not as unique as you think.

fields.title: The Remote Work Debate Is Missing the Point
fields.content: Companies demanding return-to-office and workers demanding permanent remote are both wrong because they're arguing about the wrong thing. The real question isn't where you work — it's how you work. I've seen remote teams that are incredibly productive and office teams that waste eight hours a day in meetings. I've seen remote workers who are isolated and unhappy and office workers who thrive on the energy. The answer isn't a blanket policy — it's giving teams the autonomy to figure out what works for them. Here's how we do it at my current company.

fields.title: The Best Camera Is NOT the One You Have With You
fields.content: I'm tired of this cliché. Yes, any camera is better than no camera. But if you're serious about photography, your equipment matters. Not because expensive gear makes better photos — it doesn't, automatically. But because the right tool for the job makes it possible to capture what you see. Try shooting street photography with a 200mm telephoto. Try capturing the Milky Way with a phone. Good gear won't make you a good photographer, but it will remove technical limitations that prevent good photographers from executing their vision.

fields.title: Golden Hour Is Overrated: A Defense of Harsh Light
fields.content: Every photography tutorial tells you to shoot during golden hour. The warm, diffused light is flattering, yes. But you know what's also interesting? The deep shadows and harsh contrasts of midday sun. The dramatic quality of overcast skies. The neon reflections on wet streets at midnight. Some of the most iconic photographs in history were shot in 'bad' light. Vivian Maier shot almost exclusively in direct sunlight. Fan Ho used harsh light and shadows to create geometric masterpieces. Stop waiting for golden hour and learn to see light in every condition.

fields.title: Welcome to Rare Publishing: Community Guidelines
fields.content: Welcome to Rare Publishing! We're a community of writers sharing perspectives on technology, culture, food, travel, and everything in between. A few ground rules: be respectful in comments, cite your sources when making factual claims, and remember that there's a human on the other end of every post. Posts go through an approval process before appearing publicly. If your post isn't approved within 48 hours, reach out to an admin. We're excited to read what you have to share!

fields.title: How mRNA Vaccines Actually Work: A Simple Explanation
fields.content: There's a lot of confusion about mRNA vaccines, so let's break it down without the jargon. Think of mRNA as a recipe card. Your cells are the kitchen. The vaccine delivers a recipe card that says 'make this one specific protein that looks like part of the virus.' Your cells read the recipe, make the protein, and your immune system goes 'hey, that looks suspicious' and learns to fight it. The recipe card gets destroyed after use — it doesn't change your DNA, which is like the permanent cookbook in the library. The recipe card never even goes to the library.

fields.title: Hot Take: TypeScript Is Overrated for Small Projects
fields.content: I know this is heresy in 2026, but hear me out. For projects under 5,000 lines of code with 1-2 developers, TypeScript adds more friction than value. The type system fights you more than it helps when you're prototyping rapidly. JSDoc comments give you 80% of the type safety with none of the build step complexity. I still use TypeScript for large team projects, but for side projects and prototypes? Plain JavaScript with good tests is faster and more fun.

fields.title: My Cozy Game Tier List (Fight Me)
fields.content: S Tier: Stardew Valley, Animal Crossing New Horizons. A Tier: Spiritfarer, Unpacking, A Short Hike. B Tier: Cozy Grove, Garden Story. C Tier: Ooblets (sorry). This tier list is objectively correct and I will not be taking questions. Just kidding — tell me your rankings in the comments. But seriously, if Stardew Valley isn't in your S Tier, we can't be friends.

fields.title: Controversial: Air Fryers Are Just Small Convection Ovens
fields.content: I'm ready for the hate mail. Your beloved air fryer is a convection oven with better marketing. It works by circulating hot air around food — exactly like a convection oven. The results are nearly identical. The only advantage is size — it heats up faster and uses less energy because the chamber is smaller. But calling it 'air frying' is marketing genius. You're not frying anything. You're baking. And that's fine! Baking is great! Just stop pretending it's a revolutionary technology.

fields.subject: Same experience
fields.content: We had the exact same journey at my last company. GraphQL is amazing for frontend devs but the backend complexity is real.
Have you tried tRPC? It gives you similar type safety benefits without the GraphQL complexity.

fields.subject: Great writeup
fields.content: Pinning this for our new developers. Great balanced perspective.

fields.subject: Helpful breakdown
fields.content: As someone who's been putting off learning RSC, this actually made it click for me. The mental model explanation was exactly what I needed.
Thanks Alex! The biggest aha moment for me was realizing that not everything needs to be interactive.

fields.subject: The food!
fields.content: Can confirm the konbini food is incredible. I ate 7-Eleven onigiri every morning in Tokyo and regret nothing. Also, the egg sandwiches!

fields.subject: Photography tip
fields.content: If you're into photography, Japan is a goldmine. The temples at dawn before tourists arrive are magical.
How did you handle the language barrier? That's my biggest worry about going.

fields.subject: Re: language
fields.content: Oliver — honestly, Google Translate's camera feature and basic phrases got me through 95% of situations. Most signs in cities have English too.

fields.subject: Bookmarked!
fields.content: I've been trying to recreate Prince's Hot Chicken at home for years. Trying your recipe this weekend.
The lard tip is the key. Butter doesn't get hot enough without burning.

fields.subject: Heat level?
fields.content: What heat level is this equivalent to? I want to try it but I'm a medium kind of person.

fields.subject: Re: heat level
fields.content: Jordan — this recipe is probably a 'hot' at Prince's. You can cut the cayenne in half for a solid medium.

fields.subject: What about core work?
fields.content: Great list, but shouldn't planks or ab rollouts be on here? Core stability seems pretty important.
Oliver — squats and deadlifts ARE core work. Your core stabilizes during every heavy compound movement. Direct ab work is fine but not necessary for most people.

fields.subject: Farmer's carry
fields.content: The farmer's carry is so underrated. My grip strength and posture improved dramatically after adding these.

fields.subject: Balatro!
fields.content: Balatro consumed my life for about three weeks. A poker roguelike shouldn't work that well but it absolutely does.
As someone who mostly reads about games rather than plays them, this list is really helpful. Downloading Animal Well tonight.

fields.subject: Important read
fields.content: This should be required reading. I had no idea about the resin identification code deception. That's genuinely infuriating.
This is why I started composting. At least with food waste, I know exactly where it goes.

fields.subject: Business angle
fields.content: There's actually a huge startup opportunity in plastic alternatives. Companies like Notpla are doing incredible work with seaweed-based packaging.

fields.subject: As a music producer...
fields.content: Thank you for writing this. I moved to Nashville for the music scene beyond country and it took me months to find these spots. Rudy's is the real deal.
Adding the Basement and the Basement East to this list. Both incredible venues for discovering new artists.
Grimey's is a Nashville institution. Their in-store shows are some of the best live music experiences I've ever had.

fields.subject: Thank you for sharing
fields.content: It takes courage to share failure publicly. The pricing validation point is something every founder needs to hear.

fields.subject: Tech perspective
fields.content: From a dev standpoint, the hardest lesson is that great code doesn't matter if the business model is wrong.
The farm-to-table model is tough. The logistics of cold chain delivery for local produce is a nightmare I've seen several startups struggle with.

fields.subject: Excited to be here!
fields.content: Love the community guidelines. Looking forward to sharing some tech articles!
Great platform! Can't wait to share some recipes.

fields.subject: Music section
fields.content: Is there a dedicated music category coming? Would love to write about Nashville's music scene.

fields.subject: Re: music
fields.content: Luna — yes! We just added a Music category. Write away!
